title: Introduction
text: Hey there! In this lesson, we're diving into the five execution modes that every GDS algorithm supports. These modes are crucial because they determine where your results go and how you can use them. Understanding these modes is key to building effective GDS workflows. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-7-execution-modes-s126-000-new] As you can see in this diagram, we have Estimate, Stats, Stream, Mutate, and Write. Each mode is represented by a node, and the arrows show how data and results flow differently in each mode.

title: What You'll Learn
text: By the end of this lesson, you'll be equipped to choose the right execution mode for your tasks, whether it's stream, stats, mutate, write, or estimate. You'll also learn how to build efficient workflows by strategically combining these modes. Plus, you'll get the hang of configuring algorithm parameters to control behavior and use estimate mode to plan resource requirements before running those costly algorithms.

title: The Five Execution Modes
text: Let's break down the five execution modes. First, we have **stream**, which returns results directly to your query. Then there's **stats**, which gives you summary statistics only. **Mutate** stores results in the projection, while **write** persists them to your database. Lastly, **estimate** checks memory requirements. Each mode serves a unique purpose in handling your data.

title: The Syntax Pattern
text: All algorithms follow a consistent syntax pattern. It looks like this: `CALL gds.<algorithm>.<mode>('graph-name', { configuration }) YIELD <results> RETURN <what you want>`. The mode you choose determines what gets yielded and where those results are stored. This pattern is your go-to for executing algorithms.

title: Stream Mode
text: Stream mode is all about returning results directly in your query output, without storing anything. For example, you might use it like this: `CALL gds.pageRank.stream('actor-network', {}) YIELD nodeId, score RETURN gds.util.asNode(nodeId).name AS actor, score ORDER BY score DESC LIMIT 10`. Remember, results in stream mode exist only for the duration of your query.

title: When to Use Stream
text: Stream mode is perfect for exploring results before deciding whether to store them, running one-off analyses, feeding results into other Cypher operations, or exporting to CSV or pandas via the Python driver. It's your go-to for exploration.

title: Stats Mode
text: Stats mode runs the algorithm but only returns summary statistics—no individual node results. For instance, you might call it like this: `CALL gds.louvain.stats('actor-network', {}) YIELD communityCount, modularity, ranLevels RETURN communityCount, modularity, ranLevels`. It's a quick way to get an overview.

title: When to Use Stats
text: Use stats mode when you want to understand overall algorithm behavior, test configurations before committing, check community counts or score distributions, or quickly iterate on parameter tuning. It's faster than streaming thousands of rows when you only need the summary.

title: Mutate Mode
text: Mutate mode stores results as properties in your projection—not in your database. For example, you might use it like this: `CALL gds.pageRank.mutate('actor-network', { mutateProperty: 'pageRankScore' }) YIELD nodePropertiesWritten`. These results stay in memory until you drop the graph.

title: When to Use Mutate
text: Mutate mode is great for chaining algorithms, building ML feature pipelines, keeping your database clean of intermediate results, and comparing multiple algorithm runs. It's designed for workflows, not final outputs.

title: Write Mode
text: Write mode persists results as properties in your Neo4j database. You might call it like this: `CALL gds.pageRank.write('actor-network', { writeProperty: 'pageRank' }) YIELD nodePropertiesWritten`. These results survive even after you drop the projection.

title: When to Use Write
text: Use write mode when you want to make results available to applications, share insights via dashboards, preserve results for future queries, or avoid re-running expensive algorithms. It's meant for production outputs.

title: Estimate Mode
text: Estimate mode tells you the memory requirements without actually running the algorithm. For instance, you might use it like this: `CALL gds.pageRank.write.estimate('actor-network', { writeProperty: 'pageRank' }) YIELD nodeCount, relationshipCount, requiredMemory`. Every execution mode has an estimate variant.

title: When to Use Estimate
text: Estimate mode is useful for planning before running expensive algorithms, checking if your heap can handle the operation, and deciding whether to sample or run on the full graph. It's a smart step before committing to long-running operations.

title: A Typical Workflow
text: A typical workflow might look like this: Start with **Estimate** to check memory requirements, then use **Stats** to understand the distribution and test configurations. Next, **Stream** to explore specific results, **Mutate** to chain algorithms together, and finally **Write** to persist final results. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-7-execution-modes-s140-009-new] This flowchart shows how these modes interconnect to form a complete process. You won't always use all five, but knowing when to use each makes your workflow efficient.

title: Algorithm Configuration
text: Most algorithms accept a configuration map. For example, you might call it like this: `CALL gds.pageRank.stream('actor-network', { maxIterations: 40, dampingFactor: 0.95 })`. Configuration controls how the algorithm behaves, allowing you to tailor it to your needs.

title: Universal Configuration Options
text: There are some configuration options available across most algorithms. These include `concurrency` for parallel threads, with a default of 4, `nodeLabels` to filter specific node types, and `relationshipTypes` to filter specific relationship types. These options give you flexibility in how you run your algorithms.

title: Algorithm-Specific Options
text: Each algorithm has its own unique parameters. For PageRank, you have `maxIterations`, which determines how many passes are made, with a default of 20, and `dampingFactor`, which is the probability of following links, defaulting to 0.85. For Louvain or Leiden, there's `maxLevels`, which controls hierarchy depth, with a default of 10. You'll get to know these specific parameters as you work with each algorithm.

title: Configuration Strategy
text: Here's a strategy for configuring your algorithms: Start with defaults and run without configuration. Use stats to test and check distributions and counts. Then, adjust parameters based on what you observe, and finally, validate the results to see if they answer your question. [IMAGE: img-1-foundations-7-execution-modes-s144-013-new] This flowchart outlines the steps, emphasizing the iterative nature of adjusting parameters based on observed data. Don't over-configure early; defaults are sensible starting points.

title: Summary
text: To wrap up, the five execution modes control where your results go: **Stream** for exploration, **Stats** for testing, **Mutate** for chaining, **Write** for production, and **Estimate** for planning. Configuration parameters control algorithm behavior. Start with defaults, use stats to test, and then tune as needed. In the next lesson, you'll learn how projection configuration—like undirected relationships and weights—affects algorithm behavior.
